{"step_by_step_thinking": "The patient is a 27-year-old woman with a history of hirsutism (dark coarse hair on the back), acne, irregular menstrual cycles (every 45 days), obesity (BMI calculated as 85 kg / (1.6 m)^2 = 33.6 kg/m²), and a family history of type 2 diabetes. She also has impaired glucose tolerance, as shown by a 2-hour oral glucose tolerance test result of 160 mg/dL (8.9 mmol/L), which is above the normal threshold (140 mg/dL) and indicates prediabetes or impaired glucose tolerance. Multiple documents confirm that polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS) is characterized by a combination of menstrual irregularities, hyperandrogenism (hirsutism, acne), and metabolic dysfunction including insulin resistance and glucose intolerance. The presence of these symptoms—especially the combination of hirsutism, acne, irregular cycles, and insulin resistance—is highly suggestive of PCOS. Hypothyroidism is not supported by the clinical findings, as it typically presents with dry skin, weight gain, fatigue, and bradycardia, which are not reported here. Idiopathic hirsutism is a diagnosis of exclusion and does not explain the associated acne, menstrual irregularities, or metabolic abnormalities. Ovarian hyperthecosis is a rare cause of hyperandrogenism and is not commonly associated with the full clinical picture described. PCOS is the most common cause of hyperandrogenism in women of reproductive age and fits all the clinical features described in the case.", "answer_choice": "C"}
